speaker: interviewer
text: It's December the 1st, 1995. We're conducting an interview with Leon Greenman. The interviewer is Bernice Krantz. The city is London. The country is England. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Bernice Krantz. Today's date is December the 1st, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Leon Greenman, and we're in London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We are recording.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, can you give me your name, and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Leon Greenman. L-E-O-N G-R-E-E-N-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 18th of December, 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 85 This month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't look it. Please, can you give me the city and country that you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in London. East London. Whitechapel. Bishopsgate.

speaker: interviewer
text: In England?

speaker: interviewee
text: In England. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you start the interview by telling me something about your pre-war life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'll have to go right back almost-- well, a short while after I was born. And I must start all by telling you my forefathers to make it easier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents from Mother's side were White Russian. They left White Russia, and they escaped from there when the anti-semitism was raging and the pogroms. And they arrived in London. They lived in London, and that's where my mother was born. On Father's side, I got Dutch grandparents. They were born in Amsterdam. From Amsterdam, my grandfather went to America, lived in America, and came to England and settled in London. Now what I'm talking about now is about 150 or more years ago. Grandfather-- my Dutch grandfather-- wanted to go back to Holland and spend his last few years there. At that time, the whole of the family moved away. There were six children. We're-- I'm one of the six. And the whole family moved with my grandfather to Holland. They went to Amsterdam, and we got a house in Rotterdam. I was very young. I don't remember my grandparents. I have a photograph of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old when you say you were very young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't think-- well, if I was born in 1910, probably this took place in 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were really a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. Yes. I went to school when I was six years of age in Rotterdam. So I speak the Dutch language. I left school when I was 15, passed three-- two schools, and went into the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to work first of all in a chocolate factory. And I had to sort out the chocolates from white powder. And when the governor wasn't looking, I had enough chocolate now and then. Well, after a few weeks, I didn't like the way my hair was full of white powder when I got home in the evenings, and I had enough chocolate, so I went. I got a job in a factory where they made wooden legs and wooden-- wooden tables. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your job in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the very beginning-- beginning with, I arrived at 8:00 in the morning, and the governor wasn't up yet. But-- and he left me at quarter past eight, and I had to move blocks of wood from out of the garden, which was behind the factory. It's only a little shop. And the assistant came in, and I was busy with taking the blocks of wood from out of the garden into the-- inside where the machine was. And the governor said, here are my shoes. Clean my shoes. And he gave me his shoes to clean. Now these were shiny, patent shoes. And I got a duster and took all the sawdust off. And they were really spotless. And I put them down and continued with my blocks of wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the governor called me and he said, did you clean my shoes? I said, yes. And I gave him the shoes. He looked. He said, if I tell you to clean the shoes, you use shoe polish. I said, there was no need to clean it with shoe polish. They're beautiful clean. He said, you do what I tell you to do, and otherwise, you go. So I said, well I didn't come here to have an argument about cleaning your shoes. I've come here to learn the trade, so I better go. So I took my hat and my coat, and I went. [LAUGHTER] So that job didn't last very long?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was [? less. ?] Anyhow, later on, I got a job in a printing factory, and color printing, gold printing. It all got into my hair, and my arms were full of paint in the night when you're finished. Anyhow, the first week I was there at a half a crown a week, that's one guilder 25 at that time. Well, I'm speaking about Rotterdam. And I did-- I must have done so well with my work that he gave me sixpence in a raise for my first job then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, after about 18 months, I had enough of that, and I, I left. My brothers had come over from London for a holiday, and I wanted to get back to England. I wanted to see the country where I was born. And I went with them back to England for a few week's holiday. Those few weeks became a month or more, and father was getting anxious. What is Leon doing? And my brothers at that time-- They were older than you, your brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. They were older than me. It was Charlie and Morry, yes. They were-- my brother, Morry, was a first-class tailor. He had learned the tailor business very well. And Charlie was a good upholsterer. But both left their trades and became market grafters, being their own governor on the market. And I used to go around with them, having a look around the markets, enjoying myself. Father was still in Rotterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Father wrote a letter. What's Leon doing? And my brother said, he's enjoying himself. We're looking after him. He's all right. No, said my father. He's got to learn a trade. So my brother, Morry, took me to his hairdresser in London. And I was there for about four or five months learning the trade with and without blood, how to handle a razor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a letter came from my father. Send Leon back to England-- to London-- to Rotterdam. He's got a job at my hairdresser's, and this was a first-class hairdressing saloon in Rotterdam. Doctors came there, solicitors, military men, and ministers. And I was not allowed to do-- only lathering the people. So all day long, the customer was lathering. And watching how my governor and the assistant shaved the people. And I learned shaving on the governor's face and my father's face. They were very bony, very difficult face. And I became, if I say so, a very good one in-- in shaving people, very light-handed. But I was not allowed to shave the customers. So after a while watching how my governor cut the hair of people, he said, now you've got to go out, find another saloon where you can cut customers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And practice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Practice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not on his customers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not on his customers. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went out, and I found another job. And I shaved and cut some other customers. And that went on for some years. I became a ladies' hairdresser. I took lessons in hair-- ladies' hairdressing. And I got a job in a ladies' hairdressing saloon, was a big place, about 11 people, some days, served ladies and gentlemen. How old were you about? About what age were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I was about 18, 19. And but this governor, he was very strict. I was not allowed to laugh. I was not allowed to mix or, yeah, mix with the staff and the customers, only at the seat of the customers. I did my job all right. I had to be there early in the morning to open the shop. And-- and-- and in the evening, he closed up, and then I went home and [INAUDIBLE]. I only had an hour and a half, or about an hour, for lunch. They used to call me the Flying Dutchman when I went through the streets from the saloon to my home and back again. There was not much time. Anyhow, my sister, Dinah, had a job in London. She was serving in a chemistry shop in the East End of London. And I wanted to get back to London. I'm missing a lot of this story. Anyhow, I'll come back to later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you can go back and fill in bits.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll go back there later on. And I said to the governor, I'm leaving next week. What? You're not leaving. He said, I'm opening another saloon on the other side of Rotterdam, and I wanted you to be the manager. And I said, I've had enough. I'm going to England. So I went to England. I got a job in England, ladies' hairdressing. And at 21, I had my own saloon in Bow, the East End of London. And I had an assistant, a lady assistant, helping me out and an apprentice. But both the ladies were too lazy, shall I say, to sweep up the hair or to polish the instruments. So I had to do this as well. And then I thought myself, well, this can't go on for a long time. And I sold the business. And I stuck the money into my father-- coming father-in-law, intended father-in-law, who was a bookseller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How did you meet your wife then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm coming to that now. Yes, if you want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: You met the father-in-law first? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I've got to go right back now to my hairdressing in Holland. At school when I was six, seven years of age, I-- they used to call me in front of the class to sing a song. And from that, that started really to take singing lessons later on. So I became in the Music Academy in Rotterdam later on to train my voice. Somewhere way back in Russia, as my father told me, there must have been somebody who was a cantor in shul, and probably some of that stuck on to me. So I was trying to become a concert singer. Of course, the war finished all that. But before that, I became a member of a light opera company in Rotterdam. And I also became a member of the committee of a Jewish club, boys and girls, which started with about a dozen people and grew up within little time over 100 members. And we gave monthly concerts, or some dances, something like that. But it was our duty, the members of the committee, to see the girls home. So every, every man then from the committee took six, eight, or 10 girls along, and saw them off to their homes. Now it happened during one of the concerts-- and I was on the stage singing a couple of songs-- that a young lady walked in and settled down and listened to me. And I didn't know that was to become my wife, Esther van Dam. She knew I was going to be her husband. She told me that later on, but I didn't know yet. And I was introduced to her. And she was on holiday in Rotterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: But she lived in London. She was born in Rotterdam, but she lived in London. She could-- she was working a firm making clothes for royalty. And then she left Holland for England. And I made up my mind to leave my hairdressing business and went to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spent your life going back and forth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Going backwards and forth really, yeah. So I sold my hairdressing business in England, got my intended father-in-law to know. And he had a antiquated bookshop in London, and I joined him. So we became partners. And in 1935 on the 7th of June, I married Esther van Dam. I called her Elsa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you married her in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London, the Stepney Green shul. The shul is still there. And my parents were married there. My brothers, and sister all used the same shul. And for our honeymoon, we spent the first night in Blenheim Hotel next to the pavilion in Brighton, 1935. And then we went to Holland the next day. And we intended to stay three or four weeks with my wife's grandmother, an old lady getting over 80 in Rotterdam. But during that time there, it appeared that my-- it appears that my wife was brought up since a child by this grandmother because of family circumstances. And the question was put to us, could my wife stay with her, looking after her while I was going to London, the book sale rooms, coming back, book sale rooms in-- in Holland. And that was quite all right, so we agreed. That is part of the life before the war. I want to add to that that it happened-- it must have been 1938. I found myself coming out of one of the book sale rooms in London, and I saw people digging trenches in streets, and queuing up for a gas mask. I queued up for my gas mask. I got my gas mask. And I told myself, there's something not kosher, that this is wrong. There must be a war coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I rushed back the same evening to Rotterdam to collect my wife. So when I got in, I found my wife sitting, listening to the BBC news bulletin. And we listened, and we heard Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain talking about one thing another. And then he said, consequently, there'd be no war between Germany and England. So we thought, no war? So there's no hurry to get back to England. You know what? I'll sell my stock, and then we'll go to England. Somebody else in the family can look after your grandmother. It didn't go that way at all. 1938 passed. 1939 passed. 1940 came along. And on the 17th of March 1940, our little son was born, Barney Greenman. In Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland. That was 1940. We had him registered in Rotterdam by the British Consul. And while we were doing that, we had a talk with the council, and I asked him what are the rumors of a coming war? Of course, they couldn't tell us anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, by 1940, there was already war.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Not-- not in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in Holland. No, no. But there was war in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland and Czechoslovakia, but Holland, no, that comes a little later. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you still thought that you were going to be safe there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I said, well what-- what are we to do in case there's a coming war? How are we going to get back to England? So the consul said, sign the book, A, B, or C. If you sign A, you can go in a fortnight's time to England. If you sign B, you go in a month's time. If you sign C, you go back to England with the staff of the-- of the-- of the consul. They will give you time to do what you've got to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was thinking, well, that gives me time to sell my stock. That was on the 17th of March 1940. Now what happened two months later, from 10th till the 14th of May 1940? The Nazis overrun Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rotterdam was bombed. The center of Rotterdam was totally destroyed. The school where I went to, destroyed. The streets around it was gone. The boulevards I used to walk to do my shopping-- 24 hours before that, I was walking there. Now I went along there, it was all hot ashes. The shops and the stores and mile long streets, are all [INAUDIBLE], all ruined, burned, ashes. I was walking on hot pavement.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and that was a good part of Rotterdam, and I became angry. I hated the Germans for doing that. So I decided-- I said to my wife, I don't trust the Germans. I'm here as an Englishman. I'll give my passport in safe-keeping with my non-Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now those non-Jewish friends, the son was learning, being taught voice training, the same academy of music as myself. And I used to come home with those people. So I knew them very well, and they knew me. And I gave them my passports and all the English money I had-- 758 pounds at that time. And they assured me it'd be safe. They'll look after it. I said, and when I ask for my passports, I want them back. That was assured. When the time came, I wanted my passports back, they said, we haven't got them. They said, we were afraid that the Germans would find out that we'd helped Jews, which was punishable. And safe-keeping of passports, that was certainly punishable. So we were advised to tear them up and burn them. So I was without passports. I could do nothing about it. I got in touch with the Swiss Consul in Amsterdam, and Mr. [INAUDIBLE], and asked for new passports. And I wrote letters that I'm being-- I'm being called up by the Nazis to do some work in Germany. The Nazis used to say, we're calling you up for work in Germany. In reality, it was going to the camps. But we didn't know exactly about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it took a couple of months before I got any news from the Swiss Consul, and then they sent me forms-- one for my wife, one for the child, and one for me. And we had photographs taken. I filled them all nicely in and sent them back quickly to Amsterdam. I heard nothing. So about a week later, I got permission to leave Rotterdam and go to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got into the Swiss Consul, first of all, I saw the Secretary of the Consul. And I was wearing the yellow star. That was one of the first laws the Nazis came. The Jews had to wear a star. You had to buy these, a few copies. One, you pull over one of your jacket, and one on your coat so when you go in the streets, they could tell you, that's a Jew. You had to register. You got your identity card with a J on it. You were a Jew. If you had four Jewish grandfathers, grandparents, you were a full Jew. If you had three, you were a 3/4. If you had two, you were a 1/2 a Jew. If you had one, there was a possibility that the Germans let you in peace. Well, I had four Jewish grandparents, so I was a full Jew, by-- according to the Nazis. But I was wearing the star, and all my Jewish friends in Rotterdam, they were wearing stars. So I said, I'm going to do the same as you do. But talking to the Secretary, Miss [INAUDIBLE], I told them my history. She said, you can leave that star off. What you tell me you're an Englishman. You don't need to wear a star. I said, well I'm wearing the star because I'm with my friends. I want to be with them. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stood in front of the Consul about 10 minutes later and told them my name. And he had on the table, on his desk, he had a bundle of forms. And he went right for the forms, and there, he had my three forms. And I look at him. I said, I send you these three-- those three forms a week ago, I said, and they're still on your table. I said, don't you understand? There's a-- there's a-- there's a woman and a child committed to this, my wife and child. There's some hurry with it. So he looked at me, closed the whole lot. He said, go home. It will be all right. And I went home, and it never came all right. I didn't receive anything at all from the Swiss Consul. They just didn't care.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now he is one of the five men now accused somewhat responsible for the killing of my wife and child. So I got home. Time went on. Nothing happened. I couldn't do my business anymore. Book business was dead. I had a friend who sold on the market remnants of silk and woolen cloth. And they lent me some silk and woolen cloth, sale or return. And with a little attache case, I went from house to house by Jewish people, Jewish families, see if I could sell some remnants. And you only could do that once because sometimes you came back a few weeks or later, whatever it is, and the people were taken away. Said they didn't live there no more. They were deported. So I made a little living, but not much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then comes the time I'm being called. It was one evening, about half past 5:00. I was having our dinner. Knock at the door. Open the door. We live on the first floor, so up came a man, [INAUDIBLE]. And he introduced himself, Detective so and so from the Alien Police in Rotterdam. Tell me, what are you? You're an Englishman. What are you doing here, walking around free?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'm waiting for my passports from the Swiss Consul and to be interned under Red Cross conditions. And I'm waiting. He said, well you better present yourself to the Chief Inspector at the Alien Police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Changing.
